devanagari: श्रीउद्धव उवाच । कति तत्त्वानि विश्वेश सङ्ख्यातान्यृषिभिः प्रभो । नवैकादश पञ्च त्रीण्यात्थ त्वमिह शुश्रुम ॥ १ ॥
english_devnagari: śrīuddhava uvāca kati tattvāni viśveśa saṅkhyātānyṛṣibhiḥ prabho navaikādaśa pañca trīṇyāttha tvamiha śuśruma

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]
[perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]
[imperative active second single], [imperative middle first single]

translation:
Uddhava enquired:
How many fundamental categories or principles have been enumerated by sages, O Lord of the Universe? We have heard you enumerate them as nine, eleven, five, three—in all twentyeight categories—previously.[1]

devanagari: केचित्षड्विंशतिं प्राहुरपरे पञ्चविंशतिं । सप्तैके नव षट्केचिच्चत्वार्येकादशापरे । केचित्सप्तदश प्राहुः षोडशैके त्रयोदश ॥ २ ॥
english_devnagari: kecitṣaḍviṃśatiṃ prāhurapare pañcaviṃśatiṃ saptaike nava ṣaṭkeciccatvāryekādaśāpare kecitsaptadaśa prāhuḥ ṣoḍaśaike trayodaśa

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [present middle first single], [present passive first single], [imperfect middle first single], [imperfect passive first single]

translation: Some say that the number of categories is twenty-six, others twenty-five, some others seven; some declare it to be sixteen, four and eleven respectively; while some others state that it is seventeen, some, sixteen while some others thirteen. Be pleased to explain to me, O Long-lived one, with what standpoints the sages severally declare (lit. sing) such a variety in the total number of categories.

devanagari: एतावत्त्वं हि सङ्ख्यानामृषयो यद्विवक्षया । गायन्ति पृथगायुष्मन्निदं नो वक्तुमर्हसि ॥ ३ ॥
english_devnagari: etāvattvaṃ hi saṅkhyānāmṛṣayo yadvivakṣayā gāyanti pṛthagāyuṣmannidaṃ no vaktumarhasi
translation:
Some say that the number of categories is twenty-six, others twenty-five, some others seven; some declare it to be sixteen, four and eleven respectively; while some others state that it is seventeen, some, sixteen while some others thirteen. Be pleased to explain to me, O Long-lived one, with what standpoints the sages severally declare (lit. sing) such a variety in the total number of categories.
4[2]. Though it is not supported by Reality what those Brāhmaṇas (experts in Vedas) declare is but correct as all the categories are mutually inclusive. What theory is not tenable (lit. difficult to establish) when my Māyā potency is accepted.
purport:
Footnote [2]:
Bhāgavata Candrikā: when the Brāhmaṇas accept my Māyā or Prakṛti as the basis for classification, what position is impossible and hence untenable. Hence all the positions taken by Brāhmaṇas stand to reason.

devanagari: श्रीभगवानुवाच । युक्तं च सन्ति सर्वत्र भाषन्ते ब्राह्मणा यथा । मायां मदीयामुद्गृह्य वदतां किं नु दुर्घटम् ॥ ४ ॥
english_devnagari: śrībhagavānuvāca yuktaṃ ca santi sarvatra bhāṣante brāhmaṇā yathā māyāṃ madīyāmudgṛhya vadatāṃ kiṃ nu durghaṭam

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]
[perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]
[nominative single from √ yuj class 7 verb], [accusative single from √ yuj class 7 verb]
[imperative active third dual], [imperative middle third single]

devanagari: नैतदेवं यथात्थ त्वं यदहं वच्मि तत्तथा । एवं विवदतां हेतुं शक्तयो मे दुरत्ययाः ॥ ५ ॥
english_devnagari: naitadevaṃ yathāttha tvaṃ yadahaṃ vacmi tattathā evaṃ vivadatāṃ hetuṃ śaktayo me duratyayāḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ i class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ i class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ i class 2 verb]

[nominative single from √ u class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ u class 1 verb], [accusative single from √ u class 1 verb], [nominative single from √ u class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ u class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ u class 2 verb], [nominative single from √ u class 5 verb], [vocative single from √ u class 5 verb], [accusative single from √ u class 5 verb]

translation:
The Lord replied:
“The reality is not as you stated; what I affirm is correct,” The cause of difference among such disputants is my insurmountable potencies[3] viz. Sattva, Rajas and Tamas which create innumerable mental tendencies.
purport:
Footnote [3]:
Bhāgavata Candrikā regards Prakṛti, Mahat, Ahaṅkāra, etc. as God’s powers.

devanagari: यासां व्यतिकरादासीद्विकल्पो वदतां पदम् । प्राप्ते शमदमेऽप्येति वादस्तमनु शाम्यति ॥ ६ ॥
english_devnagari: yāsāṃ vyatikarādāsīdvikalpo vadatāṃ padam prāpte śamadame

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [aorist active third single], [injunctive active third single]
[imperative active third dual], [imperative middle third single]

translation: [4] Difference (of viewpoints or theories) which becomes the subject of arguments among the disputants arises due to the cogitations (and resulting permutations and combination) of these powers. But when the control of the mind and the senses is achieved, the cause of disputation disappears and as a sequal, wrangling or discussion ceases altogether.
purport:
Footnote [4]:
According to Padaratnāvalī, wrong apprehension is due to the different powers of the Lord and the wrangling on different viewpoints is due to different attitudes of the mind. Once the mind is concentrated on the Lord through sense-control, misapprehension disappears.

devanagari: परस्परानुप्रवेशात्तत्त्वानां पुरुषर्षभ । पौर्वापर्यप्रसङ्ख्यानं यथा वक्तुर्विवक्षितम् ॥ ७ ॥
english_devnagari: parasparānupraveśāttattvānāṃ puruṣarṣabha paurvāparyaprasaṅkhyānaṃ yathā vakturvivakṣitam

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ vac ], [accusative single from √ vac ]

translation: Due to the mutual inclusiveness of the categories, the enumeration of categories varies according to the stance adopted by the speaker in explaining them as cause and effect, (resulting in the inclusion of effects into causes).

devanagari: एकस्मिन्नपि दृश्यन्ते प्रविष्टानीतराणि च । पूर्वस्मिन्वा परस्मिन्वा तत्त्वे तत्त्वानि सर्वशः ॥ ८ ॥
english_devnagari: ekasminnapi dṛśyante praviṣṭānītarāṇi ca pūrvasminvā parasminvā tattve tattvāni sarvaśaḥ
translation: Even in only one category all the other categories are found included, irrespective of their inclusion either as a cause or an effect.

devanagari: पौर्वापर्यमतोऽमीषां प्रसङ्ख्यानमभीप्सताम् । यथा विविक्तं यद्वक्त्रं गृह्णीमो युक्तिसम्भवात् ॥ ९ ॥
english_devnagari: paurvāparyamato yathā viviktaṃ yadvaktraṃ gṛhṇīmo yuktisambhavāt

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ i class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ i class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ i class 2 verb]

translation: As there is the possibility of offering a logical reasoning for the causal relations of these categories as well as their enumeration as advanced by these disputants, according to their capacity of argumentation, we, therefore accept the position presented by them.

devanagari: अनाद्यविद्यायुक्तस्य पुरुषस्यात्मवेदनम् । स्वतो न सम्भवादन्यस्तत्त्वज्ञो ज्ञानदो भवेत् ॥ १० ॥
english_devnagari: anādyavidyāyuktasya puruṣasyātmavedanam svato na sambhavādanyastattvajño jñānado bhavet
translation: As realization of the Ātman by self-effort is not possible in the case of a person possessed by nescience or ignorance which has no beginning, another person who knows the truth is essential for imparting the truth to him. (This preceptor being God as distinct from Jīva, the number of categories becomes twenty-six according to these disputants).

devanagari: पुरुषेश्वरयोरत्र न वैलक्षण्यमण्वपि । तदन्यकल्पनापार्था ज्ञानं च प्रकृतेर्गुणः ॥ ११ ॥
english_devnagari: puruṣeśvarayoratra na vailakṣaṇyamaṇvapi tadanyakalpanāpārthā jñānaṃ ca prakṛterguṇaḥ
translation: (But those who hold that the number of these principles is twenty-five and not twentysix argue:) There is not the slightest distinction between Puruṣa (Individual Soul) and Īśvara (God), as both are characterised by intelligence and consciousness). The assumption that they are mutually distinct and different is not correct. (The argument that God must be posited for imparting Self-realization is untenable as) (Self-knowledge or the faculty of knowing is inherent in sattva-guṇa which is a constituent of Prakṛti.

devanagari: प्रकृतिर्गुणसाम्यं वै प्रकृतेर्नात्मनो गुणाः । सत्त्वं रजस्तम इति स्थित्युत्पत्त्यन्तहेतवः ॥ १२ ॥
english_devnagari: prakṛtirguṇasāmyaṃ vai prakṛternātmano guṇāḥ sattvaṃ rajastama iti sthityutpattyantahetavaḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [present middle first single], [imperative middle first single]

translation: Prakṛti is nothing but the equilibrium of the attributes or modifications known as Sattva, Rajas and Tamas. They (Sattva, Rajas and Tamas) are the cause of the creation, sustenance and destruction of the universe respectively. Thus they are the attributes of Prakṛti and never that of Ātman.

devanagari: सत्त्वं ज्ञानं रजः कर्म तमोऽज्ञानमिहोच्यते । गुणव्यतिकरः कालः स्वभावः सूत्रमेव च ॥ १३ ॥
english_devnagari: sattvaṃ jñānaṃ rajaḥ karma tamo guṇavyatikaraḥ kālaḥ svabhāvaḥ sūtrameva ca
translation: In this (Sāṅkhya) system of thought, knowledge is regarded as the modification of the attribute called Sattva, action, that of Rajas and ignorance, that of Tamas. Time is the cogitating principle disturbing the equilibrium and agitating the guṇas while Nature is the Sūtra or Mahat.

devanagari: पुरुषः प्रकृतिर्व्यक्तमहङ्कारो नभोऽनिलः । ज्योतिरापः क्षितिरिति तत्त्वान्युक्तानि मे नव ॥ १४ ॥
english_devnagari: puruṣaḥ prakṛtirvyaktamahaṅkāro nabho jyotirāpaḥ kṣitiriti tattvānyuktāni me nava

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative plural from √ vac class 2 verb], [vocative plural from √ vac class 2 verb], [accusative plural from √ vac class 2 verb], [nominative plural from √ vac class 3 verb], [vocative plural from √ vac class 3 verb], [accusative plural from √ vac class 3 verb]

translation:
[5] (After elucidating the three categories mentioned (v. one above) the Lord enumerates the nine principles in that verse as follows:)
Puruṣa (The Intelligent Principle), Prakṛti (The Primordial Matter), Vyakta or Mahat (the principle of Cosmic intelligence) Ahaṃkāra (the cosmic Ego), the sky, air, fire or light, water and the earth—these are the nine categories enumerated by me.
purport:
Footnote [5]:
Bhāgavata Candrikā: Puruṣa and Prakṛti constitute the body of Paramātman. Ahaṅkāra includes all sense-organs and the five elements have in them five- objects of senses.

devanagari: श्रोत्रं त्वग्दर्शनं घ्राणो जिह्वेति ज्ञानशक्तयः । वाक्पाण्युपस्थपाय्वङ्घ्रिः कर्माण्यङ्गोभयं मनः ॥ १५ ॥
english_devnagari: śrotraṃ tvagdarśanaṃ ghrāṇo jihveti jñānaśaktayaḥ vākpāṇyupasthapāyvaṅghriḥ karmāṇyaṅgobhayaṃ manaḥ
translation: The senses of hearing, touch, sight, smell and taste—are the faculties of cognitive senses. While the organs of speech, hands, the organ of generation, the organ of excretion are the conative organs. The mind, however, is both a cognitive and conative (internal) organ, dear Uddhava.

devanagari: शब्दः स्पर्शो रसो गन्धो रूपं चेत्यर्थजातयः । गत्युक्त्युत्सर्गशिल्पानि कर्मायतनसिद्धयः ॥ १६ ॥
english_devnagari: śabdaḥ sparśo raso gandho rūpaṃ cetyarthajātayaḥ gatyuktyutsargaśilpāni karmāyatanasiddhayaḥ
translation: Sound, touch, taste, smell and colour or form—these arc the five varieties of objects of senses, while locomotion, expression or speech, excretion (including urination and defecation) and manual skill are the functions of the organs of action.

devanagari: सर्गादौ प्रकृतिर्ह्यस्य कार्यकारणरूपिणी । सत्त्वादिभिर्गुणैर्धत्ते पुरुषोऽव्यक्त ईक्षते ॥ १७ ॥
english_devnagari: sargādau prakṛtirhyasya kāryakāraṇarūpiṇī sattvādibhirguṇairdhatte puruṣo
translation: At the beginning, at the time of creation of the Universe, Prakṛti assumes the functions of both cause and effects. Through its attributes Sattva and others, it assumes the state of being created, while the unmanifest Puruṣa remains as a witness.

devanagari: व्यक्तादयो विकुर्वाणा धातवः पुरुषेक्षया । लब्धवीर्याः सृजन्त्यण्डं संहताः प्रकृतेर्बलात् ॥ १८ ॥
english_devnagari: vyaktādayo vikurvāṇā dhātavaḥ puruṣekṣayā labdhavīryāḥ sṛjantyaṇḍaṃ saṃhatāḥ prakṛterbalāt

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative plural from √ vīr class 10 verb], [vocative plural from √ vīr class 10 verb]
[nominative plural from √ vīr class 10 verb], [vocative plural from √ vīr class 10 verb], [accusative plural from √ vīr class 10 verb]

[nominative dual from √ sṛj class 6 verb], [nominative plural from √ sṛj class 6 verb], [vocative dual from √ sṛj class 6 verb], [vocative plural from √ sṛj class 6 verb], [accusative dual from √ sṛj class 6 verb], [accusative plural from √ sṛj class 6 verb]
[nominative single from √ sṛj class 6 verb], [vocative single from √ sṛj class 6 verb]

translation: The Mahat and other categories being agitated and impregnated with the energy of the glance of the intelligent principle (Puruṣa) combine together and create the egg of the Universe, reinforced with the power imparted by Prakṛti.

devanagari: सप्तैव धातव इति तत्रार्थाः पञ्च खादयः । ज्ञानमात्मोभयाधारस्ततो देहेन्द्रियासवः ॥ १९ ॥
english_devnagari: saptaiva dhātava iti tatrārthāḥ pañca khādayaḥ jñānamātmobhayādhārastato dehendriyāsavaḥ
translation: Those who hold that there are only seven categories state them as follows: Five categories beginning with the ether, knowledge i.e. knower or Jīva (Individual soul) and the Ātman (Supreme Soul), the support (of the seer and the seen, the subject and the object). The body, sense organs (and five) life breaths are evolved from these.

devanagari: षडित्यत्रापि भूतानि पञ्च षष्ठः परः पुमान् । तैर्युक्त आत्मसम्भूतैः सृष्ट्वेदं समपाविशत् ॥ २० ॥
english_devnagari: ṣaḍityatrāpi bhūtāni pañca ṣaṣṭhaḥ paraḥ pumān tairyukta ātmasambhūtaiḥ sṛṣṭvedaṃ samapāviśat

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative dual from √ yuj class 7 verb], [vocative dual from √ yuj class 7 verb], [accusative dual from √ yuj class 7 verb], [locative single from √ yuj class 7 verb]
[nominative dual from √ yuj class 7 verb], [vocative single from √ yuj class 7 verb], [vocative dual from √ yuj class 7 verb], [accusative dual from √ yuj class 7 verb]

translation: According to those who hold that the number of categories is six, they are as follows: Five elements and the sixth is the Supreme Person. Combining with the elements which are evolved (out of himself) by him, he created this universe and entered into it (as Antaryāmin, the Inner controller).

devanagari: चत्वार्येवेति तत्रापि तेज आपोऽन्नमात्मनः । जातानि तैरिदं जातं जन्मावयविनः खलु ॥ २१ ॥
english_devnagari: catvāryeveti tatrāpi teja āpo jātāni tairidaṃ jātaṃ janmāvayavinaḥ khalu

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative plural from √ jan class 1 verb], [vocative plural from √ jan class 1 verb], [accusative plural from √ jan class 1 verb], [nominative plural from √ jan class 2 verb], [vocative plural from √ jan class 2 verb], [accusative plural from √ jan class 2 verb], [nominative plural from √ jan class 3 verb], [vocative plural from √ jan class 3 verb], [accusative plural from √ jan class 3 verb], [nominative plural from √ jan class 4 verb], [vocative plural from √ jan class 4 verb], [accusative plural from √ jan class 4 verb]
[accusative single from √ jan class 1 verb], [accusative single from √ jan class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ jan class 3 verb], [accusative single from √ jan class 4 verb]

[nominative single from √ jan class 1 verb], [accusative single from √ jan class 1 verb], [nominative single from √ jan class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ jan class 2 verb], [nominative single from √ jan class 3 verb], [accusative single from √ jan class 3 verb], [nominative single from √ jan class 4 verb], [accusative single from √ jan class 4 verb]

translation: [6] (Another view mentioned in verse above holds that) the number of categories is only four. These are as follows: Fire, water and food i.e. the earth, which are born of the (fourth category viz.) Ātman. Like the evolution of things made up of parts, the creation of the universe (which too consists of numerous parts) has taken place out of these (four categories).
purport:
Footnote [6]:
It is not understood why Bhāgavata Candrikā includes the explanation of eleven categories here when a later i.e. 24th verse deals with eleven categories. According to Bhāgavata Candrikā the Ātman, five elements and five (cognitive) senses make up the eleven categories.

devanagari: सङ्ख्याने सप्तदशके भूतमात्रेन्द्रियाणि च । पञ्च पञ्चैकमनसा आत्मा सप्तदशः स्मृतः ॥ २२ ॥
english_devnagari: saṅkhyāne saptadaśake bhūtamātrendriyāṇi ca pañca pañcaikamanasā ātmā saptadaśaḥ smṛtaḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [imperative active second single], [imperative middle first single]

translation: (In verse 3 above, a view holds that the total number of categories is seventeen.) When the total number of categories is spoken of as seventeen, they are enumerated as follows; Five gross elements, five subtle elements[7] (mātrā=tan-mātrā); five (cognitive) senses, mind and the Ātman, the seventeenth, category.
purport:
Footnote [7]:
EK takes mātrā = objects of senses, EB 22.2-4.

devanagari: तद्वत्षोडशसङ्ख्याने आत्मैव मन उच्यते । भूतेन्द्रियाणि पञ्चैव मन आत्मा त्रयोदश ॥ २३ ॥
english_devnagari: tadvatṣoḍaśasaṅkhyāne ātmaiva mana ucyate bhūtendriyāṇi pañcaiva mana ātmā trayodaśa

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [imperative active second single], [imperative middle first single]

translation: When (the principles or categories are) counted as sixteen, the Ātman (the thinking Principle) is also spoken of as the mind (the rest being as in the above verse). They (number of categories) are said to be thirteen, when the five elements (Bhūtas), five senses, the mind and soul in two forms (viz. Individual soul and the Supreme Soul) are calculated together.

devanagari: एकादशत्व आत्मासौ महाभूतेन्द्रियाणि च । अष्टौ प्रकृतयश्चैव पुरुषश्च नवेत्यथ ॥ २४ ॥
english_devnagari: ekādaśatva ātmāsau mahābhūtendriyāṇi ca aṣṭau prakṛtayaścaiva puruṣaśca navetyatha

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative dual from √ aś class 5 verb], [vocative dual from √ aś class 5 verb], [accusative dual from √ aś class 5 verb]
[nominative single from √ i class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ i class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ i class 2 verb]

translation: Another view which holds that the total number of categories as eleven, is as follows: The Soul (including the mind) consists of five elements (Bhūtas) and five senses. While eight Prakṛtis (viz. mahat, ahaṅkāra, mind and five elements, combined with Puruṣa (the intelligent Principle) make up the nine categories.

devanagari: इति नानाप्रसङ्ख्यानं तत्त्वानामृषिभिः कृतम् । सर्वं न्याय्यं युक्तिमत्त्वाद्विदुषां किमशोभनम् ॥ २५ ॥
english_devnagari: iti nānāprasaṅkhyānaṃ tattvānāmṛṣibhiḥ kṛtam sarvaṃ nyāyyaṃ yuktimattvādviduṣāṃ kimaśobhanam

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [accusative single from √ kṛ class 1 verb], [accusative single from √ kṛ class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ kṛ class 5 verb], [accusative single from √ kṛ class 8 verb]

[nominative single from √ kṛ class 1 verb], [accusative single from √ kṛ class 1 verb], [nominative single from √ kṛ class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ kṛ class 2 verb], [nominative single from √ kṛ class 5 verb], [accusative single from √ kṛ class 5 verb], [nominative single from √ kṛ class 8 verb], [accusative single from √ kṛ class 8 verb]

translation: In this way different enumerations of fundamental categories have been hypothesized by old sages (to clarify the difference between Prakṛti and Puruṣa. All of them are tenable as they are cogently supported by (logical) reasoning. How can a theory proposed by the wise be unbecoming?

devanagari: श्रीउद्धव उवाच । प्रकृतिः पुरुषश्चोभौ यद्यप्यात्मविलक्षणौ । अन्योन्यापाश्रयात्कृष्ण दृश्यते न भिदा तयोः । प्रकृतौ लक्ष्यते ह्यात्मा प्...
english_devnagari: śrīuddhava uvāca prakṛtiḥ puruṣaścobhau yadyapyātmavilakṣaṇau anyonyāpāśrayātkṛṣṇa dṛśyate na bhidā tayoḥ prakṛtau lakṣyate hyātmā prakṛtiśca tathātmani

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]
[perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]

translation:
Uddhava submitted:
O Kṛṣṇa, although (as you say it) Prakṛti (Primordial nature) and Puruṣa (the intelligent Principle possess by their very nature different distinctive characteristics, (in our practical life) no difference between them is noticeable as they are mutually dependent and inter-related. (The Ātman is found in the physical body which is the product of Prakṛti while Prakṛti is found in the Ātman, as both body and Soul appear identical).

devanagari: एवं मे पुण्डरीकाक्ष महान्तं संशयं हृदि । छेत्तुमर्हसि सर्वज्ञ वचोभिर्नयनैपुणैः ॥ २७ ॥
english_devnagari: evaṃ me puṇḍarīkākṣa mahāntaṃ saṃśayaṃ hṛdi chettumarhasi sarvajña vacobhirnayanaipuṇaiḥ
translation: For the Ātman is noticed in the Prakṛti (the physical body being a product of Prakṛti), while the Prakṛti is found in the Ātman. (The very concept of “I” is impossible unless Prakṛti and Ātman are together, O lotus-eyed Lord, it behoves you to remove this grave doubt in my mind, by your decisive words supported by skilful logical reasons, O Omniscient Lord.

devanagari: त्वत्तो ज्ञानं हि जीवानां प्रमोषस्तेऽत्र शक्तितः । त्वमेव ह्यात्ममायाया गतिं वेत्थ न चापरः ॥ २८ ॥
english_devnagari: tvatto jñānaṃ hi jīvānāṃ pramoṣaste tvameva hyātmamāyāyā gatiṃ vettha na cāparaḥ
translation: It is through your grace that the individual Souls (jīvas ) receive enlightenment. It is due to your power (called Avidyā or Nescience) that knowledge of the Jīvas is obscured. You alone and nobody else knows the course and working of your own Māyā.

devanagari: श्रीभगवानुवाच । प्रकृतिः पुरुषश्चेति विकल्पः पुरुषर्षभ । एष वैकारिकः सर्गो गुणव्यतिकरात्मकः ॥ २९ ॥
english_devnagari: śrībhagavānuvāca prakṛtiḥ puruṣaśceti vikalpaḥ puruṣarṣabha eṣa vaikārikaḥ sargo guṇavyatikarātmakaḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]
[perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]
[imperative active second single], [perfect active first single], [perfect active second plural], [perfect active third single]

translation:
The Lord replied:
There is a fundamental difference between Prakṛti[8] (the psycho-physical organism called the body) and Puruṣa (the Soul), O eminent Uddhava. This (body) is subject to changes (such as boy-hood, youth, old age, etc.) as it is created out of the disturbance of the balance of guṇas (while the Soul is changeless and self-existent).
purport:
Footnote [8]:
SS., Bhāgavata Candrikā and Padaratnāvalī interpret Prakṛti in a broad sense as ‘the created universe’.
(i) Bhāgavata Candrikā: This created universe consisting of Adhibhūta, Adhidaiva and Adhyātma, is evolved out of Ahaṃkāra which in turn is the evolute of Prakṛti. Ahaṃkāra as applied to an individual is a synthesis of Ātmic (or psychic) qualities expressing love, hate and physical qualities like corpulence, etc.
(ii) Padaratnāvalī: There is a cardinal difference between Prakṛti and Puruṣa. The confusion between the two is due to misapprehension caused by Tamo- guṇa. The universe is a creation of the combination of guṇas in different proportions, with a particular guṇa dominating the whole combination.

devanagari: ममाङ्ग माया गुणमय्यनेकधा विकल्पबुद्धीश्च गुणैर्विधत्ते । वैकारिकस्त्रिविधोऽध्यात्ममेकमथाधिदैवमधिभूतमन्यत् ॥ ३० ॥
english_devnagari: mamāṅga māyā guṇamayyanekadhā vikalpabuddhīśca guṇairvidhatte vaikārikastrividho

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ vidh class 6 verb], [vocative single from √ vidh class 6 verb], [accusative single from √ vidh class 6 verb]
[nominative single from √ vidh class 6 verb], [vocative single from √ vidh class 6 verb], [accusative single from √ vidh class 6 verb]

translation: Dear Uddhava my Māyā which is constituted of three guṇas, creates through them manifold differences and concepts about them, in various ways. The creation which is subject to modifications is of thiree types—Adhyātma, Adhidaiva and Adhibhūta.

devanagari: दृग्रूपमार्कं वपुरत्र रन्ध्रे परस्परं सिध्यति यः स्वतः खे । आत्मा यदेषामपरो य आद्यः स्वयानुभूत्याखिलसिद्धसिद्धिः ॥ ३१ ॥
english_devnagari: dṛgrūpamārkaṃ vapuratra randhre parasparaṃ sidhyati yaḥ svataḥ khe ātmā yadeṣāmaparo ya ādyaḥ svayānubhūtyākhilasiddhasiddhiḥ
translation: (For example) The sense of sight is Adhyātma The form or colour (i.e. the object seen) is Adhibhūta and the aṃśa or part of the sun present in the occular sense is Adhidaiva. These are interdependent (anyone of them depends on the other two) for its existence. But not so that (the sun) which exists independently in the sky. In the same way the Soul (Ātman) who is the cause of the Adhyālma etc. is different from them. It is by virtue of his own inherent power of consciousness that he illumines other agents (such as Adhyātma) which shed light on others. Similar (threefold) nature can be predicated about the senses of touch, hearing, smell and taste and of the mental faculties (ego, etc.)

devanagari: एवं त्वगादि श्रवणादि चक्षुर् । जिह्वादि नासादि च चित्तयुक्तम् ॥ ३२ ॥
english_devnagari: evaṃ tvagādi śravaṇādi cakṣur jihvādi nāsādi ca cittayuktam

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ yuj class 7 verb], [accusative single from √ yuj class 7 verb]

translation: Ahaṃkāra (the ego) is the modification caused by the agitation of guṇas (as a result of the disturbance of their equilibrium) caused by the Time-spirit or the Supreme Ruler (who is thus an instrumental cause). It is an evolute of the Mahat, of which Prakṛti is the material cause. It is of three types viz. Vaikārika (from Sattva guṇa), Aindriya (from Rajo-guṇa) and Tāmasa (from Tamo-guṇa). It is the Ahaṃkāra which causes the notion of difference and delusion.

devanagari: योऽसौ गुणक्षोभकृतो विकारः प्रधानमूलान्महतः प्रसूतः । अहं त्रिवृन्मोहविकल्पहेतुर्वैकारिकस्तामस ऐन्द्रियश्च ॥ ३३ ॥
english_devnagari: yo ahaṃ trivṛnmohavikalpaheturvaikārikastāmasa aindriyaśca

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ kṛ class 1 verb], [nominative single from √ kṛ class 2 verb], [nominative single from √ kṛ class 5 verb], [nominative single from √ kṛ class 8 verb]

translation: The Ātman is essentially pure knowledge. The controversy about the existence or non-existence of difference (between the Soul and the body or Ahaṃkāra is due to nonrealization of the self. Though it has no basis and is meaningless (as it lasts till self-realization), it is true that it will not disappear from the minds of those who are averse to me—their very self.[9]
purport:
Footnote [9]:
Padaratnāvalī: Such people presume existence for the non-existence and vice- versa. Perceiving what is contrary to reality these people identify Jīva with Īśvara and deny the reality of the universe (A dig at Advaitavāda). According to Padaratnāvalī—difference between Jīva, Īśvara and the reality of the universe is true, but the opponents deny it and hence the controversy.

devanagari: आत्मापरिज्ञानमयो विवादो ह्यस्तीति नास्तीति भिदार्थनिष्ठः । व्यर्थोऽपि नैवोपरमेत पुंसां मत्तः परावृत्तधियां स्वलोकात् ॥ ३४ ॥
english_devnagari: ātmāparijñānamayo vivādo hyastīti nāstīti bhidārthaniṣṭhaḥ vyartho

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ mad class 1 verb], [nominative single from √ mad class 3 verb], [nominative single from √ mad class 4 verb]

translation:
Uddhava said:
Be pleased to explain to me how persons whose minds have turned away from you, accept (are born into) and give up (in death) various bodies high or low as determined by their own Karmas, O Govinda. This phenomenon is very difficult to comprehend (properly in all its aspects) by persons of poor understanding. Generally there is a scarcity of persons who know this as all people are deluded (by your Māyā).[10]
purport:
Footnote [10]:
Padaratnāvalī thinks that the purpose of Uddhavas’ asking this question is to know if there is any other cause of the transmigration of the soul.
(nityānāṃ jīvānāṃ janma-mṛtī kīdṛṣyau, karmetara kāraṇaṃ kim iti pṛcchati uddhavaḥ).

devanagari: श्रीउद्धव उवाच । त्वत्तः परावृत्तधियः स्वकृतैः कर्मभिः प्रभो । उच्चावचान्यथा देहान्गृह्णन्ति विसृजन्ति च ॥ ३५ ॥
english_devnagari: śrīuddhava uvāca tvattaḥ parāvṛttadhiyaḥ svakṛtaiḥ karmabhiḥ prabho uccāvacānyathā dehāngṛhṇanti visṛjanti ca

translation:
Uddhava said:
Be pleased to explain to me how persons whose minds have turned away from you, accept (are born into) and give up (in death) various bodies high or low as determined by their own Karmas, O Govinda. This phenomenon is very difficult to comprehend (properly in all its aspects) by persons of poor understanding. Generally there is a scarcity of persons who know this as all people are deluded (by your Māyā).[10]
purport:
Footnote [10]:
Padaratnāvalī thinks that the purpose of Uddhavas’ asking this question is to know if there is any other cause of the transmigration of the soul.
(nityānāṃ jīvānāṃ janma-mṛtī kīdṛṣyau, karmetara kāraṇaṃ kim iti pṛcchati uddhavaḥ).

devanagari: तन्ममाख्याहि गोविन्द दुर्विभाव्यमनात्मभिः । न ह्येतत्प्रायशो लोके विद्वांसः सन्ति वञ्चिताः ॥ ३६ ॥

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative plural from √ vid class 2 verb], [vocative plural from √ vid class 2 verb]
[nominative plural from √ vañc ], [vocative plural from √ vañc ]
[nominative plural from √ vañc ], [vocative plural from √ vañc ], [accusative plural from √ vañc ]

translation:
The Lord explained:
The mind of men as modified by the subtle impressions left by karmas and accompanied by the five (subtle) senses, transmigrates from one region (i.e. body) to another and the Soul though distinctly different from them, follows (due to the influence of Ahaṃkāra.)

devanagari: श्रीभगवानुवाच । मनः कर्ममयं नॄणामिन्द्रियैः पञ्चभिर्युतम् । लोकाल्लोकं प्रयात्यन्य आत्मा तदनुवर्तते ॥ ३७ ॥
english_devnagari: śrībhagavānuvāca manaḥ karmamayaṃ nṝṇāmindriyaiḥ pañcabhiryutam lokāllokaṃ prayātyanya ātmā tadanuvartate

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]
[perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]
[accusative single from √ yu class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ yu class 6 verb], [accusative single from √ yu class 9 verb]

[nominative single from √ yu class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ yu class 2 verb], [nominative single from √ yu class 6 verb], [accusative single from √ yu class 6 verb], [nominative single from √ yu class 9 verb], [accusative single from √ yu class 9 verb]
[nominative single from √ yu class 3 verb], [accusative single from √ yu class 3 verb]

translation: The mind is under the dominance of (the residual impressions left by) Karmas. According to their influence the mind (at the time of death) broods over the objects actually experienced (during the life-time) or heard (as promises of future fruits of karmas as stated in scriptures. (After death) he becomes manifest (i.e. is reborn) among the objects contemplated by him and the previous objects (in the last existence) are lost in oblivion. Thereafter, the memory connecting the previous existence with the present birth becomes lost.

devanagari: ध्यायन्मनोऽनु विषयान्दृष्टान्वानुश्रुतानथ । उद्यत्सीदत्कर्मतन्त्रं स्मृतिस्तदनु शाम्यति ॥ ३८ ॥
english_devnagari: dhyāyanmano udyatsīdatkarmatantraṃ smṛtistadanu śāmyati

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ sad class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ sad class 1 verb], [accusative single from √ sad class 1 verb]

translation: Due to excessive attachment to the present body and objects of enjoyment or due to some mysterious reason a person does not remember (anything about) his body, etc. of the) last existence. Such total obliviousness is verily the death of a being. (It is the body that is forgotten and dead and not the Soul).

devanagari: विषयाभिनिवेशेन नात्मानं यत्स्मरेत्पुनः । जन्तोर्वै कस्यचिद्धेतोर्मृत्युरत्यन्तविस्मृतिः ॥ ३९ ॥
english_devnagari: viṣayābhiniveśena nātmānaṃ yatsmaretpunaḥ jantorvai kasyaciddhetormṛtyuratyantavismṛtiḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [accusative single from √ man class 4 verb], [accusative single from √ man class 8 verb]
[nominative single from √ man class 4 verb], [accusative single from √ man class 4 verb], [nominative single from √ man class 8 verb], [accusative single from √ man class 8 verb]
[nominative single from √ i class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ i class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ i class 2 verb]

translation: And they (scriptures) opine that the (new) birth of a being consists of the identification on the part of the jīva with the objects of enjoyment i.e. with a particular body just as one imagines oneself identical with the body in one’s dreams or in a day-dream (forgetting that his body in the wakeful condition is different, O liberal Uddhava.

devanagari: जन्म त्वात्मतया पुंसः सर्वभावेन भूरिद । विषयस्वीकृतिं प्राहुर्यथा स्वप्नमनोरथः ॥ ४० ॥
english_devnagari: janma tvātmatayā puṃsaḥ sarvabhāvena bhūrida viṣayasvīkṛtiṃ prāhuryathā svapnamanorathaḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [instrumental single from √ man class 4 verb], [instrumental single from √ man class 8 verb]

translation: In. this manner, in the present dream state, the jīva does not remember his previous dreams or reverie and looks upon himself as a newly born person, though as a matter of fact he is the same old already existent person.

devanagari: स्वप्नं मनोरथं चेत्थं प्राक्तनं न स्मरत्यसौ । तत्र पूर्वमिवात्मानमपूर्वं चानुपश्यति ॥ ४१ ॥
english_devnagari: svapnaṃ manorathaṃ cetthaṃ prāktanaṃ na smaratyasau tatra pūrvamivātmānamapūrvaṃ cānupaśyati

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative dual from √ smṛ class 1 verb], [vocative dual from √ smṛ class 1 verb], [accusative dual from √ smṛ class 1 verb], [locative single from √ smṛ class 1 verb]

translation: When a new body which is the site of the ten sense-organs, is born or the mind, the basis of the senses, identifies itself with a new body (in a different birth), the threefold distinction (viz. a particular body being the highest, the middling and the lowest, Or of being in the wakeful, dreamy or sleepy state, Or the body, the mind and senses) though unreal, becomes apparent in the Ātman who alone is real. Due to false identification with them, the Ātman becomes the cause for the idea of internal and external differences (i.e. He enjoys external objects of senses and experiences internal (mental) states such as pleasure, pain etc.). Even as an individual Soul (Jīva) creates (out of himself) a number of unreal bodies and sees himself as many in a dream or the father of a wicked son, though himself impartial, looks upon differently with the friends and foes of his wicked sons.

devanagari: इन्द्रियायनसृष्ट्येदं त्रैविध्यं भाति वस्तुनि । बहिरन्तर्भिदाहेतुर्जनोऽसज्जनकृद्यथा ॥ ४२ ॥
english_devnagari: indriyāyanasṛṣṭyedaṃ traividhyaṃ bhāti vastuni bahirantarbhidāheturjano
translation:
(In order to confirm Uddhava’s feeling of renunciation, the Lord adds unasked).
It is every moment that bodies of creatures come into being and go out of existence, through the force of Time of inexorable velocity. But the velocity is too subtle to be perceptible to gross senses.

devanagari: नित्यदा ह्यङ्ग भूतानि भवन्ति न भवन्ति च । कालेनालक्ष्यवेगेन सूक्ष्मत्वात्तन्न दृश्यते ॥ ४३ ॥
english_devnagari: nityadā hyaṅga bhūtāni bhavanti na bhavanti ca kālenālakṣyavegena sūkṣmatvāttanna dṛśyate
translation: Just as in the cases of changes (in size of) flames, the flow of the current of a stream and changes in the (green or unripe, yellowish or ripe) appearances of fruit, so in the case of all created beings, changes in age and other factors (such as physical strength, lust, skill, etc. are brought about by (the continuous current of) Time.

devanagari: यथार्चिषां स्रोतसां च फलानां वा वनस्पतेः । तथैव सर्वभूतानां वयोऽवस्थादयः कृताः ॥ ४४ ॥
english_devnagari: yathārciṣāṃ srotasāṃ ca phalānāṃ vā vanaspateḥ tathaiva sarvabhūtānāṃ vayo

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative plural from √ kṛ class 1 verb], [vocative plural from √ kṛ class 1 verb], [nominative plural from √ kṛ class 2 verb], [vocative plural from √ kṛ class 2 verb], [nominative plural from √ kṛ class 5 verb], [vocative plural from √ kṛ class 5 verb], [nominative plural from √ kṛ class 8 verb], [vocative plural from √ kṛ class 8 verb]

[nominative plural from √ kṛ class 1 verb], [vocative plural from √ kṛ class 1 verb], [accusative plural from √ kṛ class 1 verb], [nominative plural from √ kṛ class 2 verb], [vocative plural from √ kṛ class 2 verb], [accusative plural from √ kṛ class 2 verb], [nominative plural from √ kṛ class 5 verb], [vocative plural from √ kṛ class 5 verb], [accusative plural from √ kṛ class 5 verb], [nominative plural from √ kṛ class 8 verb], [vocative plural from √ kṛ class 8 verb], [accusative plural from √ kṛ class 8 verb]

translation: (Even though the luminous particles in a flame appear and disappear every moment) the statement is made that ‘This is the same lamp’, (and although particles of water flow down the current giving place to other ones at the same spot in the stream of water) it is asserted that ‘this is the same water of the stream’,[11] similarly when the persons whose life is a waste (due to ignorance) say, ‘He is the same man,’ their notion and talk is unreal (for changes take place in the body every moment. The notion of unchanging absolute identity is incorrect).
purport:
Footnote [11]:
This bears close comparison to the doctrine of flux attributed to the Greek philosopher Heraclitus (500 b.c.). As Plato quotes it in Theaetetus. “You cannot step twice into the same river; for fresh waters are ever flowing in upon you”.—B, Russel: A History of Western Philosophy, p. 45 (11th Paperback edt. 1965).

devanagari: सोऽयं दीपोऽर्चिषां यद्वत्स्रोतसां तदिदं जलम् । सोऽयं पुमानिति नृणां मृषा गीर्धीर्मृषायुषाम् ॥ ४५ ॥
english_devnagari: so so
translation: Even this (ignorant) person, is neither born nor dead due to the seed of his karmas. The Ātman is immortal. It is an illusion to predicate birth and death in the case of Ātman, as in the case of fire (which survives to the end of the Kalpa but) which is regarded as enkindled or extinguished due to the limiting condition of the piece of wood with which it is associated.

devanagari: मा स्वस्य कर्मबीजेन जायते सोऽप्ययं पुमान् । म्रियते वामरो भ्रान्त्या यथाग्निर्दारुसंयुतः ॥ ४६ ॥
english_devnagari: mā svasya karmabījena jāyate so mriyate vāmaro bhrāntyā yathāgnirdārusaṃyutaḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [present middle third single], [present passive third single]

translation: Entry into the womb, existence in the womb, birth, childhood (upto the age of five), boyhood (upto the age of sixteen), youth (upto the age of fifty), middle age (upto the age of sixty), old age and death—These are the nine stages of the body.

devanagari: निषेकगर्भजन्मानि बाल्यकौमारयौवनम् । वयोमध्यं जरा मृत्युरित्यवस्थास्तनोर्नव ॥ ४७ ॥
english_devnagari: niṣekagarbhajanmāni bālyakaumārayauvanam vayomadhyaṃ jarā mṛtyurityavasthāstanornava
translation: These fanciful states of the body (which are not related to the Soul), both high and low, are identified as one’s own due to associations with Guṇas. It is seldom that someone disowns them and casts them off (through the Grace of God).

devanagari: एता मनोरथमयीर्हान्यस्योच्चावचास्तनूः । गुणसङ्गादुपादत्ते क्वचित्कश्चिज्जहाति च ॥ ४८ ॥
english_devnagari: etā manorathamayīrhānyasyoccāvacāstanūḥ guṇasaṅgādupādatte kvacitkaścijjahāti ca
translation: One’s own birth can be inferred from that of one’s son and death, by that of one’s father. But he who clearly understands that birth and death are related to the body, this knower is the Ātman who is not subject to and characterized by both (birth and death).

devanagari: आत्मनः पितृपुत्राभ्यामनुमेयौ भवाप्ययौ । न भवाप्ययवस्तूनामभिज्ञो द्वयलक्षणः ॥ ४९ ॥
english_devnagari: ātmanaḥ pitṛputrābhyāmanumeyau bhavāpyayau na bhavāpyayavastūnāmabhijño dvayalakṣaṇaḥ
translation: Just as the knower of the sprouting of a plant and of its end, is different from the plant itself, so also the Ātman who is the seer, is distinct from the body.

devanagari: तरोर्बीजविपाकाभ्यां यो विद्वाञ्जन्मसंयमौ । तरोर्विलक्षणो द्रष्टा एवं द्रष्टा तनोः पृथक् ॥ ५० ॥
english_devnagari: tarorbījavipākābhyāṃ yo vidvāñjanmasaṃyamau tarorvilakṣaṇo draṣṭā evaṃ draṣṭā tanoḥ pṛthak
translation: An ignorant person does not distinguish his Ātman from his body in this way and becomes infatuated with the object of the senses and identifies himself with the body, and thus becomes a subject of transmigration or Saṃsāra.

devanagari: प्रकृतेरेवमात्मानमविविच्याबुधः पुमान् । तत्त्वेन स्पर्शसम्मूढः संसारं प्रतिपद्यते ॥ ५१ ॥
english_devnagari: prakṛterevamātmānamavivicyābudhaḥ pumān tattvena sparśasammūḍhaḥ saṃsāraṃ pratipadyate
translation: He wanders in the whirligig of Saṃsāra by being born as a sage or a god by doing actions dominated by sattva- guṇa; by his association with rajas he is born among Asuras or human beings, and through association with tamas, he goes to the state of evil spirits or sub-human beings.

devanagari: सत्त्वसङ्गादृषीन्देवान्रजसासुरमानुषान् । तमसा भूततिर्यक्त्वं भ्रामितो याति कर्मभिः ॥ ५२ ॥
english_devnagari: sattvasaṅgādṛṣīndevānrajasāsuramānuṣān tamasā bhūtatiryaktvaṃ bhrāmito yāti karmabhiḥ
translation: Just as one imitates the artistes while one witnesses a dance or attends a musical performance, in the same way the Ātman though free from desires or inactive, witnesses the qualities of Ahaṃkāra and is prompted to imitate them.

devanagari: नृत्यतो गायतः पश्यन्यथैवानुकरोति तान् । एवं बुद्धिगुणान्पश्यन्ननीहोऽप्यनुकार्यते ॥ ५३ ॥
english_devnagari: nṛtyato gāyataḥ paśyanyathaivānukaroti tān evaṃ buddhiguṇānpaśyannanīho

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [accusative plural from √ nṛt class 4 verb], [ablative single from √ nṛt class 4 verb], [genitive single from √ nṛt class 4 verb]
[ablative single from √ nṛt class 4 verb], [genitive single from √ nṛt class 4 verb]

translation: Just as trees (though stationary) on the bank of a flowing stream of water appear to be moving or when the eye is set rolling, the earth appears to be rolling; or just as reveries and drcam visions though unreal give the pleasurable and painful experience to one, similarly, the saṃsāra that is the pleasures or the pains experienced therein arc unreal, O descendent of Dāśārha.

devanagari: यथाम्भसा प्रचलता तरवोऽपि चला इव । चक्षुषा भ्राम्यमाणेन दृश्यते भ्रमतीव भूः ॥ ५४ ॥
english_devnagari: yathāmbhasā pracalatā taravo cakṣuṣā bhrāmyamāṇena dṛśyate bhramatīva bhūḥ
translation: Just as trees (though stationary) on the bank of a flowing stream of water appear to be moving or when the eye is set rolling, the earth appears to be rolling; or just as reveries and drcam visions though unreal give the pleasurable and painful experience to one, similarly, the saṃsāra that is the pleasures or the pains experienced therein arc unreal, O descendent of Dāśārha.

devanagari: यथा मनोरथधियो विषयानुभवो मृषा । स्वप्नदृष्टाश्च दाशार्ह तथा संसार आत्मनः ॥ ५५ ॥
english_devnagari: yathā manorathadhiyo viṣayānubhavo mṛṣā svapnadṛṣṭāśca dāśārha tathā saṃsāra ātmanaḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative plural from √ dṛś class 1 verb], [vocative plural from √ dṛś class 1 verb]
[nominative plural from √ dṛś class 1 verb], [vocative plural from √ dṛś class 1 verb], [accusative plural from √ dṛś class 1 verb]

translation: Though from the point of reality, worldly objects have no existence, the saṃsāra does not vanish in the case of a person who broods over them, just as a person experiences evils in a dream.

devanagari: अर्थे ह्यविद्यमानेऽपि संसृतिर्न निवर्तते । ध्यायतो विषयानस्य स्वप्नेऽनर्थागमो यथा ॥ ५६ ॥
english_devnagari: arthe hyavidyamāne dhyāyato viṣayānasya svapne

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect active first single], [perfect active second plural], [perfect active third single]

translation: Therefore, O Uddhava, do not enjoy the objects of pleasure with the senses which are by nature evil (unreal). Please understand that it is merely an illusion which shows difference which is reflected in the Ātman due to ignorance about its true nature.

devanagari: तस्मादुद्धव मा भुङ्क्ष्व विषयानसदिन्द्रियैः । आत्माग्रहणनिर्भातं पश्य वैकल्पिकं भ्रमम् ॥ ५७ ॥
english_devnagari: tasmāduddhava mā bhuṅkṣva viṣayānasadindriyaiḥ ātmāgrahaṇanirbhātaṃ paśya vaikalpikaṃ bhramam
translation: Even if scolded, insulted, defrauded, envied, belaboured, fettered, deprived of the means of livelihood or defiled with actual spitting and urination and thus persecuted in various ways by the wicked and the ignorant, and thus subjected to a difficult situation, an aspirant after Final beatitude should extricate his Ātman by dint of his own self.

devanagari: क्षिप्तोऽवमानितोऽसद्भिः प्रलब्धोऽसूयितोऽथ वा । ताडितः सन्निरुद्धो वा वृत्त्या वा परिहापितः ॥ ५८ ॥
english_devnagari: kṣipto tāḍitaḥ sanniruddho vā vṛttyā vā parihāpitaḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ kṣip class 4 verb], [nominative single from √ kṣip class 6 verb]

translation: Even if scolded, insulted, defrauded, envied, belaboured, fettered, deprived of the means of livelihood or defiled with actual spitting and urination and thus persecuted in various ways by the wicked and the ignorant, and thus subjected to a difficult situation, an aspirant after Final beatitude should extricate his Ātman by dint of his own self.

devanagari: निष्ठ्युतो मूत्रितो वाज्ञैर्बहुधैवं प्रकम्पितः । श्रेयस्कामः कृच्छ्रगत आत्मनात्मानमुद्धरेत् ॥ ५९ ॥
english_devnagari: niṣṭhyuto mūtrito vājñairbahudhaivaṃ prakampitaḥ śreyaskāmaḥ kṛcchragata ātmanātmānamuddharet
translation:
Uddhava submitted:
Please explain to me in such a way as I may understand it, O prominent among the eloquents. I consider this outrage of the wicked on my self as simply unbearable.

devanagari: श्रीउद्धव उवाच । यथैवमनुबुध्येयं वद नो वदतां वर ॥ ६० ॥
english_devnagari: śrīuddhava uvāca yathaivamanubudhyeyaṃ vada no vadatāṃ vara

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]
[perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]
[present middle first single], [present passive first single]
[imperative active third dual], [imperative middle third single]

translation:
Nature (or Attachment to one’s body) is too powerful even for learned people, (they will certainly revolt against such treatment) except in the case of those who are absorbed in performing the (course of) duties enjoined by you, and have become quiescent by securing asylum at your feet,
[back to top]

devanagari: सुदुःसहमिमं मन्य आत्मन्यसदतिक्रमम् । विदुषामपि विश्वात्मन्प्रकृतिर्हि बलीयसी । ऋते त्वद्धर्मनिरतान्शान्तास्ते चरणालयान् ॥ ६१ ॥
english_devnagari: suduḥsahamimaṃ manya ātmanyasadatikramam viduṣāmapi viśvātmanprakṛtirhi balīyasī ṛte tvaddharmaniratānśāntāste caraṇālayān

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [present middle first single], [present passive first single]
[nominative plural from √ śam class 4 verb], [vocative plural from √ śam class 4 verb], [nominative plural from √ śam class 9 verb], [vocative plural from √ śam class 9 verb]

[nominative plural from √ śam class 4 verb], [vocative plural from √ śam class 4 verb], [accusative plural from √ śam class 4 verb], [nominative plural from √ śam class 9 verb], [vocative plural from √ śam class 9 verb], [accusative plural from √ śam class 9 verb]
